text:
The article delves into the science behind why some people sneeze louder than others. The first point is the biological explanation of sneezing. According to Mas Takashma, the chair of the Department of Otolaryngology, Head and Neck Surgery at Houston Methodist Academic Institute, our nose functions as an air filter for the lungs. It contains a mesh of epithelial cells, tiny hairs, and thick mucus that trap particulates to protect the lungs. When these particulates accumulate, they need to be expelled, and this is done by sneezing, a process facilitated by a sneeze gene and a small compound in our nasal cavity. The sneeze signal is processed by the hippocampus in the brain, leading to reflexive muscle contractions. This protective mechanism helps flush out old cells and aids the immune system in protecting from viruses.

The second point focuses on the volume of a sneeze, which is determined by the structure of our respiratory system. The sneeze reflex begins with deep inhalation, followed by the closure of our vocal cords as air is sucked into our lungs. Once enough pressure is built up, the air is expelled, creating the sound of the sneeze. The shape and "floppiness" of our vocal cords and other soft tissue at the back of the throat, as well as lung volume, influence the sound produced during a sneeze.

Lastly, the article discusses the societal and cultural factors influencing the sound of a sneeze. Takashma points out that in Japan, where there is a cultural emphasis on not inconveniencing others, people tend to suppress their sneezes. However, sneeze suppression can cause medical issues such as nose bleeds and sinus fractures. Keeping the mouth closed is the most effective way to sneeze quietly. The influence of societal norms, cultural factors, and psychological aspects on the sound of a sneeze highlights the complexity of this seemingly simple bodily function.
